speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So where was your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think you-- pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: The doctor was in the other part of the hospital. And doctors, they started-- they treated her. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Ukrainian sister told me this, and how I begged her to keep her if she survives. And she said to me, where did you come from with her? I said, from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, you can't go back to the ghetto. Because if she will live, she needs her mother. It's not what you said to take-- we don't take children. But we want you to survive. If you survive, then you'll have your daughter-- if you both survive. Don't go back to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I said, that is my family. Where can I go? Find someone. She was very good. They all-- people were good to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Find someone [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] outside the ghetto, someone who will help you, and come back here in a few days later and find out about your daughter, what will happen to her. We don't promise you. We have no food here. The Germans are not giving us food. And the Germans are coming every day, because there were children from before the war with illnesses that they had to stay a long time there. And if boys, the Germans come, and they take them straightaway. We can't do much. You know how it is. Gestapo comes, and they look through the beds, the children. If there is a little boy, a Jewish boy, they take him out, and they kill them. Girls are a little bit luckier because they don't know exactly who is Jewish, who is not. We can keep some children here. We don't have here many Jewish children. But we'll see what will happen to your child. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I went back. I went back to the ghetto. In, you could go. But out, not. Some people went out, from the committee. So they could-- because they communicated with the Germans, they could go out and go into the ghetto. I went in. They didn't know who I am. Some non-Jews could get in if they have something-- to deliver something. It was at the begin.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went in. And my sister, my other sister, brother-in-law-- is it you? They couldn't believe it. No one could believe that I went there to-- thinking I gave up the child to the hospital and came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't like here. You go to a hospital. You deliver a child to a hospital. It was miles and miles in nighttime in wartime. And I said to her, all right, I came to see if you're all alive. We'll see what will happen. But I told them what that sister told me in the hospital. Then I didn't think of it. I remembered-- that saved me-- that my younger sister, the one who survived-- I think I told you her story too. When that Polish woman gave her a document out of a certificate of a church and told her to go to Lublin with it-- and from Lublin, she can somehow go to Germany or let herself to get caught on the street and sent to Germany as a Christian. She had that paper. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She begged that Christian-- that woman, the same woman who gave her-- said, look, I have an older sister, older from me. And she said, she's quite good looking. She doesn't look like Jewish. Even better, because I was fair and she was dark. Help her too. But she has a little child. That's when I still had the child in the ghetto. That happened in the ghetto before my daughter got sick, in the first two weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said, and I begged her to take you in. And she said, good, I'll give her a document too, for her. She went to the church and got out.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a few days with her before she went to Lublin, got to the church. I don't know if it was another church. And got out that birth certificate for me, her sister's, whom she sent away to-- to Brody, to another city. Otherwise, we would be two the same names, the same-- not the same age, because I was-- her sister was older. In the papers, I was older than I am, a few years older. That wouldn't matter. And she went there, gave me-- and gave my sister, for me, that birth certificate from the-- from another church where her sister was christened. But she said, all right, she can come to me, not with the child, without the child. I won't take her with the child. My sister said, what will happen will happen. First, I have the certificate for her. And she gave it to her. My sister gave it to me. I put it in my pocket-- I remember exactly, I had a green coat-- into my coat pocket, and forgot about it. And meanwhile, my sister left for Lublin. And somehow-- difficulties, of course. She was afraid, scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, she got to Germany as a non-Jewess. And she was about 30 kilometers from me in Germany. I never knew that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The youngest is 30 kilometers from me, she was, in another DP-- not DP. DP was later. A forced labor camp. And we never knew about each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she left. And when I was at the hospital with my child, I didn't remember that I had it in my pocket. I got through so much that I forgot about that paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came back to the ghetto and I told the story to my sister-- although, to my older sister and brother-in-law, I never thought of it. And when I said, she said, go out. Maybe someone will help you amongst non-Jews, because if your child will survive, she needs her mother. And you have to live too-- not in the ghetto. Don't go back to the ghetto. When I said that, I remembered I have it in pockets, this. I went out. But that woman told my sister, before she left-- she told her that I should come to her about 10 o'clock or 11 o'clock nighttime, because she used to be on that little market on [INAUDIBLE] selling bread, where she got to know my sister, selling her bread. She said I should come late in the evening. She doesn't want anyone to see me there. Where she lived-- [POLISH] 26. So many years, I remembered. When I talk, it comes back. [POLISH], it was off [? Grodetska. ?] [POLISH], the side of [? Grodetska. ?] And her name was [? Glenbrodska. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: First name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Yusefa ?] [? Glenbrodska. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Yusefa. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Yusefa ?] [? Glenbrodska. ?] And she had two children, a girl of about 11, a boy about 6. And she said, let her come-- without the child, let her come to me about 10 o'clock. Then I come back from the market. All day she was there, feeding her children there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did remember at that time. I forgot it. It will come back. It will. I'll remember them, because I used to teach them to read Polish and write Polish. That's why she took me in. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I were to go on the streets. I had that paper. But I've heard before, from other people in the ghetto, that if you show it-- if you are caught by Germans on the street and you take out the paper, show them, it's not good. Because all Jews did it to cover themselves straightaway. Don't show anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I kept this here. I didn't show it to anyone. All day, I walked around. And no one bothered me on the streets. 10 o'clock, I went to her place. But she was not still-- she said, when I come home, you'll hear my children talking loud. They talk loud. You'll recognize that's-- see someone, that's me and my children. She lived-- it was a square-- I don't know-- like a courthouse, square around little buildings. In the front, there was-- in a square, like [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a big building. And a heavy entrance floor-- entrance door to open, to go in. And inside, you went in, and the buildings were not in the center like normally, but like a courthouse and the sides around. Hers was opposite. She described it to my sister so I would know where to go. Nighttime, I have no idea. If I go into another place, they would straightaway know it's not one that they knew, but a stranger. Opposite-- to go in there. And there is a little entrance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Low, very low-- very poor people lived there, very poor. The floors were not even boards. It was a-- just a soil, you know? Not covered at all inside, very poor, a table and a bed, that was all. And what to do? I went in. I can't go out on the street at night. I thought maybe someone will see me. So I thought, the best I go into a toilet. They had outdoor toilets, wooden outdoor-- not like here in the flats. Not at that part. It was a very poor part.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- a outdoor-- we have here a toilet outside, there to the back where the carports are. There is a toilet too. Like I say, it's a wooden toilet. There was such a toilet. I thought I'll wait in the toilet. And it was near, not far from her place. So I can hear the children talking when she comes home. I knew when she has to come, between 10:00

speaker: interviewee
text: 00, half past 10:00. I went to the toilet. But then someone wants to go to the toilet from those houses around. There were people living.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a man went in-- want to go in. So he started-- in Ukraine, he something said-- something not nice. And he opened and had seen-- it was dark, no light there. But he knew it's a woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to talk because if I talk-- if I don't talk, it's someone from there. I went out. He went in. He finished his business, went out. He went out and was right into his place. I went back. There was only one toilet for all the people around. I went back to the toilet. Another one came. (LAUGHING) I had to go out again. I went out. I didn't go for any purpose in. But I hid there. Another one-- went out. This one didn't know that I was before. So he went out. And I went out. He went in. I didn't talk. And he finished and went out. As he went out, I could hear children coming. So it was a relief. And as he went out, I went up to them quickly. I said, I'm [? Christina's ?] sister. Come, come, quick, quick. Dragged me into there-- came into that place. Awful, terrible. All clothing-- heaps on the wall-- on the floor. The floors, there were no boards, nothing. One bed was here and a bed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, all right, but where's your child? In hospital. That was-- I was lucky that it happened that I had to leave her to the hospital. So I came to her without the child. And she took me in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Took me in, and she said, look, I go to the market every day. It's not a market like here. It's a little place. It was a few benches.

speaker: interviewee
text: I go to the market every day. And I come back late. I haven't got food here, nothing. And you haven't got either. How will you live here? I said, don't worry. You have water here? Yes. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived on water. I don't know how I survived. For years-- years-- I didn't eat, nothing. I was thin like a skeleton. That's another thing. In the morning, she left. But she came home earlier and cooked a bit of black coffee. She gave me too. Her children ate there with her. She brought them some sausage and bread she had. But she didn't bring home bread. And she said, look, you can stay with me if you teach my children Polish, because not with the Russians and not Germans. They were closed schools-- all closed, no schools [INAUDIBLE] at all. So she said, teach my children to read and write. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think the girl was [? Krisha, ?] and the boy was [? Vladek. ?] It comes everything back. I never thought of it. I started to teach. And nothing went into their heads. Another thing, she was a little woman like this. She had once syphilis. So half a nose, half they cut off. It was syphilis. She didn't have it now. Her husband left her. And she was alone with the two children. She was very poor. Why she saved my sister and saved me-- [INAUDIBLE] later. She saved my little daughter too, because she wanted us to convert to Christianity. She was devoted Christian. And she didn't do it for me. She did it for Jesus Christ. She told me. I'll take you to the church. And you'll become a Christian. I said, all right, good. And you'll be my sister. All right, I said. Everything's all right. But she didn't take me straightaway. From there, something else. I had to go every day to the hospital-- not I had to. I wanted to see if my daughter's alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So how to go? I had courage. I had more than I needed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just went on the street like nothing. I believed I'm not Jewish. I even started to pray to Jesus, Maria. I know songs. The children sang it. And I sang it too. I know the songs till now. I can tell you in Polish, word by word, songs, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: So I believed in it. And I went on the street firmly, crossed everywhere as a non-Jewess. I believed that I'm not.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought I'll never go back to Jews. I don't need them. The Jewish God is not helping us.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish God had seen my little brother beaten to death, and my mother, and my father, and my sisters, and all of them, and all Jews. Why didn't he help us? I don't believe it anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is no God and no-- maybe-- maybe the non-Jewish religion will help me. Maybe they will help. They live. And we are killed, all. Maybe they will-- he will help me. And I went on the street believing that I'm not Jewish. So I went, and no one bothered me. If I have seen a Gestapo, I went straight. Some people, if they were Jews under assumed names, they ran away if they've seen-- and they knew it. And they ran away. I went straight forward. And he smiled. And I smiled back. Often they smiled to me, a Polish girl and maybe still good looking then. So they smiled. Oh, I went straight into him. And so I was on streets many times. And so I went that long way to the hospital. And as I came there, they still said, we don't know. We still don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything? Is she a little better? We don't know. Nothing is the same.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, as I went-- yes. I still have left-- and I kept it. I didn't spend it, not on me-- not a few-- like, a few pennies I had left somewhere in a pocket. I got them out. And somewhere there I bought a tiny, little roll. I don't know if even she could eat it-- to bring to her a little something to eat. And I brought it. And I went there. And as I went there, I gave it to the sister. I said, give it to her. They didn't have bread. They didn't have food for the children in the hospital-- milk, nothing, nothing, really nothing. I don't know how those children survived at the hospital, because they didn't give them anything, the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I gave it to the sister. And said she will give it to her. Much, much later, she told me, when she was out of the hospital and she could talk-- because a long time after the hospital, she couldn't talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: But many years later, even-- when I found her later, after so many years, she said, I put it under my pillow. And other children got it away from me. They've seen me hiding it under my pillow. And they took it away from me. And some suspected that she's Jewish. I don't know why. So I went, day after day, day after day, to the hospital to find out. Finally, I think it was about two and a half weeks later, I came. And she sister said to me, yes. She is not 100%. But we don't keep children longer. She's better. It won't come back. She's out of danger. You can take her home. I said, I want to see her. First, I don't take her now because we were not allowed to travel on trams. And there were no Jews on the streets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before, even, Jews were not allowed to travel in trams. And there were no Jews on the streets. How can I carry her such a long way back, and if someone will recognize me on the street? So I said, yes, can she be picked up tomorrow after tomorrow? They said, yes, a few days she can still stay here. But I want to see her. I couldn't get in because that was infectious. She said, you see people there at the window, a row of standing people looking through the window? On the other side of the hospital, the nurse will bring you to her-- or a sister. And you will see her through the window. I mean, not a open window-- closed. But we can look in. Yes. So I waited before the window. And I waited and waited. And there were mothers. They have seen their children smiling, laughing. And everybody left. I was alone, standing alone. The other side stood a nurse with a little boy without hair in her arms. And I said through the window, where's my child? She said-- showed here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, that's not my child. It's a mistake. That's not my child. Yes. And I looked closer. And that little child with no hair, her face like that finger, with tiny little lips, like narrow, white, tiny little lips smiled to me. And that was my child. But she got through so much, and no food at all, that I couldn't recognize her. She recognized me. I was the same, even very thin. But she was not the same. I showed you a picture. I think you have seen it. That is when she was nourished already after the war. It was like this with a face like that, the sunken eyes, no hair at all. They had to shave her head. And white-- a tiny little white face with tiny little two white lips. And she smiled. She recognized me. And I thought, if she smiles to me, it is my child. And they took her away. And I went home. I went back to the Polish woman, where I stayed with her. And I fell on my knees. I really-- I just fell before and said, listen, do with me what you want. Go and bring my child from the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: You said you won't take me with my child. What can I do? I offer her, I said, to Jesus, Maria, Jesus and Maria, and myself too.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can-- you can go with us tomorrow to the church, with me and with her. But go and bring her home. I can't bring her. I carried her once there. You can go by tram. I couldn't. And I can't take her home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said, all right, if you promise that we'll go tomorrow to the church. She was devoted Christian. She was Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you promise you-- I said, yes, I told you many times. I believe already now, even if I don't go. If I'm not converted, I believe already. All right, come on. And we all went to the church. And she took the child. She brought her back to my place-- to-- to her place. She took her home. The child couldn't speak still from diphtheria. She was still not well. She needed food desperately. She couldn't give her anything. She didn't. If she could-- that only I have against her. She fed her children on that little market. But she didn't bring home any food, and she didn't give her to give me. Just as she was like this, I went out. And she quarreled with me. I still had a few pennies, and I wanted to buy a little bit of milk for her. How much I could buy-- such a-- just a few centimeters in a cup. She quarreled later with me that I have money but didn't want to give it to her, the woman. I said I didn't have-- that what I have looked through, I haven't got. That what I-- and I kept it for her. I said, you give to your children too, and she has nothing. I'll tell you later about her too. I went out. And as I went out, I didn't even say goodbye to her, nothing to my child. I didn't say where I'm going. I said, I'll come back soon. They rounded up on the-- the Gestapo rounded up-- they looked for a black market. It was illegal. And there were people coming from the country selling a little bit of milk or a bit of butter. And that was illegal. And the Germans surrounded us all and took us to prison for that. I was-- first, not to the prison. I'll tell you something else. They rounded. And I was amongst them. I never went back to the child. From there, they took us to a Ukraine militsiya. It was like a police not far from where I lived there. They called it militsiya, Ukraine militsiya. They took us to the-- they rounded up. And there were, I would say, about 80 to 100 people. There they started to question us.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was like-- all the time, I was dressed poorly, like all the others. I wasn't different from other country people like them. I had a skirt around and a blouse on like them. There, we stood there. They started to question us. Where did you come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they asked all others until they came to me-- two militsiya men, Ukrainians. They were worse than the Gestapo, much worse. They had-- we were in the first room as we entered. There was another room at the back. I have seen they took in-- I don't know-- from the street somewhere, a Jewish girl, a beautiful, blond Jewish girl, maybe 16, 17. They took her in-- into straightaway. They knew she is Jewish. I don't know why and how they found out. Maybe somewhere near the ghetto. I don't know where. They took her into the other room. There were about 10 or 12 militsiya men, Ukrainian, big, heavy men. They all raped her-- all. And then-- or maybe she agreed to let her live, you know, to pay for-- for life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then after all, she's-- when they said no or something like it, she ran out. And as she ran out, she was-- she ran. She wanted to run away. Where? I don't know where she wanted to run. There was nowhere to run.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the street she was, out from one door. And I was the second door, that entrance door. They shot after her. And they shot her in the leg. And she fell. What happened to her, I don't know. She still lived, but shot in the leg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside we were. And they started questioning us. They asked me, where did you come from? They were Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, from Brody. It's a town further away. I know that there were no telephones stringed to Brody to find out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? I said, because I brought in a little bit of milk to sell. I wanted a bit of money. They didn't say anything else.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one Jew amongst them I recognized straightaway. And they gave us, because we were not Jews-- we're as-- as people from the country selling, you know-- illegally selling milk and butter. They gave us-- they cooked there somewhere a bit of soup-- everybody, me too, as a non-Jewess. They gave us a little bit of soup. When I had that little bit of soup, I didn't want even to eat it. I had no appetite. There was one, he was as a non-Jew too-- a man standing. He recognized me. A Jew recognizes-- others not. But a Jew recognize the other one. They didn't give him, because he was a man. I don't know. Because they suspected that he's Jewish. They didn't give him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he showed me. I took my soup, and I gave it to him. I gave it to him. And he ate it. Then after a while, they took us all together. There was a big-- a truck, you know? The trucks for-- for-- for horses, or-- that they-- from place to place, they carry. They loaded us all into that truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't say where we go. That went to the prisons. They sent us all. They didn't release anyone, the whole group, as we waited. On that, we had to go up into that truck. As we went into the truck, that Jew, he wanted to-- that's how people are silly-- stupid. I always-- I said to you before, I wait till the last minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said-- thought to me-- to myself, if I run, you know or get out of the truck while the truck was in motion-- traveling, you know? I'm not going to jump. He jumped out, he was shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there. And I saw where we are going, the streets. I recognized all the streets. We went up to that famous-- it was very famous. The street was [NON-ENGLISH]. And the prison was called [NON-ENGLISH]-- up to there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.
